skill.name: latchbio-integration
skill.description: Guides users through building, configuring, and deploying bioinformatics workflows on the Latch platform using the Latch SDK.
skill.summary: Build Latch bioinformatics workflows with SDK, data, resource, and verified workflow guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are markdown examples, CLI snippets, public links, and Latch SDK usage notes rather than executable skill code. No evidence found of malicious prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or hidden runtime behavior. The skill is low risk because it documents commands, cloud workflow execution, local file handling, and secret retrieval that users should run only in trusted projects.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The reported shell execution findings are markdown command examples and inline formatting. They document installing, initializing, registering, and executing Latch workflows, and they are not executable code inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced markdown examples, not Ruby backtick execution or hidden shell calls. The commands are normal Latch CLI usage for this skill domain.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Appears In Example Workflow Code
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings are example snippets showing how Latch tasks read local input files, write output files, and use temporary task storage. This is expected for bioinformatics workflow documentation, but users should review paths before running generated workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are contained in documentation examples and are limited to ordinary workflow input, output, and temporary storage paths. No skill file performs these operations automatically.

low_findings.title: Secret Retrieval Example Requires User Care
low_findings.description: The environment access finding is a Latch SDK secret retrieval example using get_secret("api_key"). This is a legitimate secure-secret pattern, but generated workflows should avoid logging or returning secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows only a secret retrieval example and no network transmission or logging of the value. The residual risk is user misuse when adapting the example.

low_findings.title: Public URLs And Promotional Link Are Not Exfiltration Evidence
low_findings.description: The network findings are public documentation, repository, community, blog, and K-Dense Web links. No evidence found that the skill sends local data, credentials, or workspace content to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or product links, and there is no executable request code. The K-Dense Web suggestion may influence user behavior, so it remains a low marketplace concern.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import And Weak Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import findings correspond to normal Python import examples, and the weak cryptography findings appear to be scanner matches on unrelated bioinformatics text and decorators. No cryptographic operation or dynamic loader was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The verified workflow lines show a static from-import statement. The cited SKILL.md lines describe verified bioinformatics workflows, not cryptography.

content.value_statement: Bioinformatics teams need reproducible workflows without managing cloud infrastructure. This skill explains how to create Latch SDK pipelines, configure resources, manage data, and use verified workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to define Latch workflows with @workflow and task decorators.
Shows LatchFile and LatchDir patterns for cloud data inputs and outputs.
Documents resource options for CPU, memory, GPU, storage, and timeouts.
Covers registration paths for Python, Nextflow, and Snakemake workflows.
Summarizes verified workflows for RNA-seq, DESeq2, AlphaFold, and single-cell analysis.
Describes Registry concepts for projects, tables, records, links, and enums.
limitations: Requires a Latch account, Latch SDK setup, and Docker for real workflow registration.
Provides guidance and examples, not a live connection to the Latch platform.
Example workflow code must be adapted and tested with real data and dependencies.
Platform limits, pricing, and verified workflow availability may change outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Create a research workflow
use_cases.description: Build a Python workflow that processes sequencing files and returns managed Latch outputs.

use_cases.title: Deploy an existing pipeline
use_cases.description: Plan how to register a Nextflow or Snakemake pipeline on Latch with required inputs and outputs.

use_cases.title: Select compute resources
use_cases.description: Choose CPU, memory, GPU, storage, and timeout settings for costly analysis tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Latch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Latch workflow for one input file and one output file. Explain each decorator and type annotation.

prompt_templates.title: Add cloud data handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to use LatchFile and LatchDir for input data, intermediate results, and final outputs in a sequencing workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Tune workflow resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend Latch task resource settings for a workflow with quality control, alignment, quantification, and report generation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a production pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Latch RNA-seq workflow that uses Registry records, verified workflow components, launch plans, and cost-aware resources.

output_examples.input: Create a Latch workflow for FASTQ quality control.
output: Defines a small task for quality control.
Uses LatchFile for the input FASTQ file.
Returns a managed output file in Latch storage.
Notes that Docker and Latch login are required before registration.

output_examples.input: Help me configure GPU resources for AlphaFold.
output: Identifies AlphaFold as a GPU-suitable workload.
Recommends a GPU task decorator or custom task resource settings.
Explains when to choose V100 or A100 capacity.
Adds cost and timeout checks before deployment.

output_examples.input: Organize sample metadata for a Latch workflow.
output: Creates a Registry plan with projects, tables, and records.
Maps sample identifiers, conditions, and file references.
Uses linked records for results connected to samples.

best_practices: Test workflow logic locally with representative data before registering it on Latch.
Use the smallest resource decorator that meets runtime and memory requirements.
Keep secrets in managed secret storage and never print secret values in workflow logs.
anti_patterns: Do not register unreviewed workflows that execute commands from untrusted user input.
Do not overuse GPU or large-memory tasks when a standard task is sufficient.
Do not store long-term results only in temporary task storage.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps design Latch bioinformatics workflows, data handling patterns, resource settings, and deployment steps.

faq.question: Can it run Latch workflows for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You still need a Latch account, Docker, and the Latch SDK to run workflows.

faq.question: Does it support Nextflow and Snakemake?
faq.answer: Yes. The references describe how Latch can register existing Nextflow and Snakemake pipelines.

faq.question: Can it help with GPUs?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains standard GPU decorators and custom CPU, memory, GPU, storage, and timeout settings.

faq.question: How should secrets be handled?
faq.answer: Use managed secret retrieval patterns and avoid logging, returning, or hardcoding secret values in workflow code.

faq.question: Is this only for RNA-seq?
faq.answer: No. It also covers verified workflows for protein structure, single-cell analysis, CRISPR analysis, and general data management.
